---
format: html
editor: visual
---

# Statics Review {#sec-statics}

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Learning Objectives

-   Define external and internal force and moment reactions
-   Calculate external reactions
-   Determine internal pin and two-force member reactions for structures composed of connected members
-   Determine internal reactions in continuous bodies
-   Calculate reactions in three dimensions
:::

All bodies and structures discussed in this text are assumed to be in static equilibrium, which means they experience no acceleration (the sum of all forces and moments are equal to zero). Any body that is not moving or that is moving at constant velocity is in static equilibrium. However, unlike in a standard statics course—where all bodies are assumed to be rigid—in this text bodies are expected to be deformable. This means they may stretch, contract, twist, bend, break, buckle, and so on. To determine how an applied loading situation affects a given body or structure—and potentially causes it to deform—we must first apply statics to establish the distribution of forces and moments within the body. This is the first step of many problems. This chapter reviews those aspects of statics that are prevalent throughout this course.

@sec-1.1 reviews the concept of external loads and their representation on free body diagrams.

@sec-1.2 reviews equilibrium of structures in two dimensions, including analysis of two-force members and multiforce members.

@sec-1.3 addresses internal loads, including normal force, shear force, and bending moment.

@sec-1.4 reviews equilibrium in three dimensions.

## External Reactions and Free Body Diagrams {#sec-1.1}

<details>

<summary>Click to expand</summary>

External forces and moments are those that act on the boundaries of a system. They include loads applied to the main structure (weight, wind, pressure, etc.) and the reactions these loads induce in supporting elements (pins, rollers, welds, etc.). In the case of external reactions, the term *reaction* refers to the forces and/or moments exerted by the supports on the body in response to the applied loading to maintain equilibrium.

Finding the external reactions is the first step in many of the problem types covered in this text. This process begins with drawing a free body diagram (FBD) of the body. An FBD is a sketch of the body "freed" from its supports, showing all the applied and reaction forces and moments to be considered in the analysis. In cases where some loads are insignificant compared to others (e.g., weight sometimes falls into this category), those loads are omitted from the FBD.

The importance of drawing FBDs as a first step in calculating internal and external loads and reactions cannot be overstated. Creating these diagrams establishes which loads and reactions are being included in the analysis, as well as their assumed directions. With the diagrams prepared, you can maintain consistency when summing forces and moments to apply equilibrium equations, helping to ensure accurate results.

Some commonly encountered supports, along with their corresponding reactions and FBD representations, include the following:

-   **Pin supports**: Pin supports resist any lateral movement of the body relative to the pin at its location. The direction of the overall reaction force (F) is generally unknown, so it is represented as a set of perpendicular force components (F~x~ and F~y~) in the figure below but may also be represented as any set of perpendicular components.

![Pin supports](images/CH1%20PNGs/table%201.1%20part%201.png){#fig-1.1 fig-alt="Three free body diagrams represent the same beam with a vertical cut on the right. The first diagram includes a pin support at the bottom-left corner. The second replaces the support with an internal force arrow F at the right end, pointing northeast, with angle θ measured from the horizontal. The third shows F resolved into horizontal and vertical components, F sub x and F sub y, forming a right triangle at the cut." fig-align="center" width="525"}

-   **Normal supports**: Normal supports generally consist of surfaces on which the body simply rests (like a beam on a roller) and/or supports that are not fixed to any other surface (like a rocker). Similar to pin supports, normal supports prevent movement of the body relative to the support at its location. However, unlike pins, normal supports prevent only movement in the direction normal to the support site. Consequently, the reaction force (F) acts strictly perpendicular to the supporting surface.

![Normal support](images/June%2013%20figure%20edits/chapter%201%20unnumbered.png){#fig-1.2 fig-alt="Top: Two free body diagrams represent the same beam with a vertical cut on the right end. On the left the beam has a roller support and the other a rocker support, with the word “OR” placed between them. These are set equal to a beam with the same cut, where the support is replaced by an internal force arrow labeled F pointing upward, located at the left end of the beam. Bottom: Two free-body diagrams show the same beam resting on a smooth surface that slopes downward from left to right. An equal sign in the middle shows that the two diagrams represent the same situation. On the left, the beam touches the sloped surface. On the right, the surface is replaced by a dashed line and a single reaction force F that acts perpendicular to the slope, pointing upward and to the right." fig-align="center" width="505"}

-   **Cable**: A cable provides a tensile force reaction (T). It pulls on the body but never pushes. The reaction force acts in a direction along the path of the cable, extending away from the body and toward the external attachment point.

![Cables](images/CH1%20PNGs/table%201.1%20part%203.png){#fig-1.3 fig-alt="Two free body diagrams represent the same beam with a vertical cut on the right end. On the left, an external cable is attached to the top-left corner of the beam, angled upward and to the right (northeast). This is set equal to the same beam with the support replaced by an internal tensile force arrow labeled T at the right end, pointing in the same direction as the cable (northeast)." fig-align="center" width="376"}

-   **Fixed support**: A fixed support is capable of resisting both lateral movement and rotation, so its reactions consist of a reaction force (F) and a reaction moment couple (M). As with the pinned support, the direction of the overall reaction force is not necessarily known and is therefore represented by a set of perpendicular components. It is important to remember that the reaction moment couple to be solved for is an unknown, just like the reaction force components, and must be included in the moment equation about any point on the body.

![Fixed supports](images/CH1%20PNGs/table%201.1%20part%204.png){#fig-1.4 fig-alt="Three rectangular free body diagrams represent the same beam with a vertical cut on the right end and are set equal to each other. The first diagram includes a fixed support at the left end. The second replaces the fixed support with internal forces at the left end: an arrow labeled F pointing upward and to the right (northeast), and a counterclockwise internal moment. The third diagram includes the same moment, with F resolved into horizontal and vertical components labeled F sub x and F sub y, forming a right triangle." fig-align="center" width="513"}

Note that when components of the reaction force are found individually, as with the pin support and fixed support, the overall magnitude of the force (F) can be calculated as

$$
F=\sqrt{F_x^2+F_y^2}\text{.}
$$

Its direction (i.e., angle measured from the horizontal axis) can be calculated as

$$
\tan \theta_x=\frac{F_y}{F_x}\text{.}
$$

Note how the FBDs are drawn in the examples provided throughout this chapter. Geometrical details of a body—such as lengths and angles—are often included on FBDs. However, in this text these features are frequently excluded because they are provided in the problem statement, and the focus here is on representing the loading. You should keep in mind that in some courses and with some instructors these geometrical details may be considered required features of FBDs.

</details>

## Equilibrium in Two Dimensions {#sec-1.2}

<details>

<summary>Click to expand</summary>

Once the FBD is drawn, the next step is to apply the equilibrium equations. In two dimensions (x-y plane) these are

$$
\begin{align}
\boxed{\sum F_x=0 \quad\sum F_y=0 \quad
\sum M_{any~point}=0}
\end{align}
$$ {#eq-1.1}

A review of finding moments is given in @sec-1.4.

Since there are three equations, a statically determinate problem should have no more than three unknowns.

@exm-1.1 illustrates the process of finding external reactions.

::::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}
## Example 1.1

:::: {#exm-1.1}
A 3 ft beam is supported by a pin connection at the wall at point A and a cable at point C as shown. A load is applied 2 ft from point A.

Find the force in pin A as well as the tensile force in the cable.

![](images/chapter%201%20figure%20updates/example%201.1%20part%201.png){fig-alt="A 3-foot-long rectangular beam is supported by a pin connection at the wall on the top-left corner, labeled point A. Point C is located 3 feet to the right of point A, where a cable extends upward and to the left (northwest), forming a 30-degree angle with the beam. A downward point load of 1200 pounds is applied 2 feet to the right of point A." fig-align="center" width="310"}

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}
## Solution

**Step 1: Draw the FBD.**

The beam is supported with a pin at A, so the support is represented on the FBD with force components A~x~ and A~y~. Note that an assumption must be made about whether A~x~ points right or left and whether A~y~ points up or down. The actual directions of A~x~ and A~y~ are determined by whether the calculated values are positive or negative. A positive value means the assumed direction was correct, and a negative value means the force acts in the opposite direction.

Cables always exert a force that pulls away from the body. In the FBD below, the cable is represented by force T.

![](images/chapter%201%20figure%20updates/example%201.1%20part%202.png){fig-alt="Free body diagram of a 3-foot rectangular beam. The pin support at point A from Example 1.1 is replaced by internal reaction forces: A sub x, an arrow pointing left, and A sub y, an arrow pointing upward. At point C, an external tensile force labeled T is shown as an arrow pointing upward and to the left (northwest), forming a 30-degree angle with the beam. A 1200-pound downward point load is applied 2 feet to the right of point A." fig-align="center" width="317"}

**Step 2: Apply equilibrium equations.**

If we start with the moment about A equilibrium equation, we can solve for the tensile force T since A~x~ and A~y~ do not contribute to the moment about A. Then substituting T into the two-force equilibrium equations allows us to find A~x~ and A~y~.

$$
\begin{aligned}& \sum M_A=T \sin (30^{\circ}) * 3{~ft}-1,200{~lb }*2{~ft}=0\\[10pt]& T = 1,600 {~lb}\\[20pt]& \sum F_x=-A_x-T \cos (30^{\circ})=0\\& -A_x-1,600{~lb} \cos (30^{\circ})=0\\& A_x=-1,385.6{~lb} \\[20pt]& \sum F_y=A_y+T \sin (30^{\circ})-1,200{~lb}=0\\& A_y+1,600{~lb} \sin (30^{\circ})-1,200{~lb}=0\\& A_y= 400{~lb}\end{aligned}
$$

Since A~x~ is negative, it acts in the opposite direction from the assumed direction on the FBD. Thus, A~x~ acts in the positive x direction.

The total force in pin A is

$$
F_A=\sqrt{A_x^2+A_y^2}=1,442{~lb}\text{.}
$$

**Answer:**

T = 1,600 lb

F~A~ = 1,442 lb
:::
::::
:::::

### Two-Force Members {#sec-1.2.1}

One special type of pin connection for which the direction of the reaction force is known is one in which the pin is connected to a two-force member. Contrary to its name, a two-force member is not necessarily a member on which only two forces act. Rather, it is a member where forces are applied at only two distinct locations regardless of how many forces there are. A two-force member can have any shape, as is demonstrated in @fig-1.5. One easy way to recognize a two-force member is by noting the presence of only two pinned points and no other locations where a force or moment couple is applied. Once a member is identified as a two-force member, it can be concluded that the resultant force at both pins is equal in magnitude (F~A~ = F~B~ in @fig-1.5), is opposite in direction, and follows a line of action that goes through the pins. For a straight member, the internal force (discussed in @sec-1.3) is equal to the reaction force in the pin.

![Illustrations of two force members showing the line of action of the reaction force (red arrows) due to applied forces (black arrows) passing through the joints. Note that F~A~ = F~B~.](images/chapter%201%20figure%20updates/figure%201.1.png){#fig-1.5 fig-alt="Four shapes illustrate different two-force members, each with point A and point B connected by a dashed line through the member. First shape: A rectangle oriented diagonally, with point A at the bottom left and point B at the top right. Red arrows labeled F sub A and F sub B extend outward from A and B along the dashed line, indicating equal and opposite reaction forces. Three black arrows point toward A and three toward B, representing applied forces. Second shape: A two-segment member shaped like an inverted L, made of two rectangular bars joined at a right angle. Point A is at the end of the horizontal segment on the left, and point B at the bottom of the vertical segment. Red arrows labeled F sub A and F sub B point toward A and B along the dashed line, indicating equal and opposite reaction forces. Three black arrows point toward A and three toward B. Third shape: A vertical rectangle with a short base and longer height. Point A is at the top left and point B at the bottom right. Red arrows labeled F sub A and F sub B extend outward from A and B along the dashed diagonal line, indicating equal and opposite reaction forces. Three black arrows point toward A and three toward B. Fourth shape: A curved member shaped like an inverted elbow, with point B at the top and point A at the bottom. Red arrows labeled F sub A and F sub B point toward A and B along the dashed diagonal line, indicating equal and opposite reaction forces. Three black arrows point toward A and three toward B." fig-align="center" width="542"}

The presence and recognition of two-force members simplifies equilibrium calculations by reducing the number of unknowns before any computations are performed. This is demonstrated in @exm-1.2.

::::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}
## Example 1.2

:::: {#exm-1.2}
Determine the force in pin C and in pin A.

![](images/Updated%20CH1%20examples/example%201.2%20part%201.png){fig-alt="A two-member structure composed of connected rectangular bars. Member AB is inclined upward to the right at a 50° angle from the horizontal and is 5 meters long. Member BC is horizontal and 4 meters long. A downward force of 4000 N acts at the midpoint of BC. The structure is supported by pin connections at A and C, with a pin joint at B connecting the two members." fig-align="center" width="440"}

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}
## Solution

**Step 1: Draw the FBD.**

![](images/Updated%20CH1%20examples/example%201.2%20part%202.png){fig-alt="Free body diagram of the two-bar structure from Example 1.2. A downward arrow labeled 4000 N is applied at the midpoint of the horizontal bar BC. Reaction forces are shown at the pin supports: A sub x and A sub y at point A, and C sub x and C sub y at point C." fig-align="center" width="472"}

Given this FBD, it appears that there are four unknowns, so it can not be solved with only three equilibrium equations. However, recognizing that bar AB is a two-force member (since there is a pin at A and a pin at B but no other forces acting on that bar), we know that the line of action of the reaction force at A goes through points A and B. Thus, the FBD can be redrawn with only three unknowns.

![](images/Updated%20CH1%20examples/example%201.2%20part%203.png){fig-alt="Free body diagram of the two-bar structure. A downward arrow labeled 4000 N is applied at the midpoint of the horizontal bar BC. Reaction forces at point C are shown as C sub x and C sub y. A single force arrow labeled F sub A is drawn along the axis of the inclined bar (northeast), replacing A sub x and A sub y to represent the bar as a two-force member." fig-align="center" width="474"}

**Step 2: Apply equilibrium equations.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \sum M_C=F_A \cos \left(50^{\circ}\right)*(5{~m}) \sin \left(50^{\circ}\right)-F_A \sin \left(50^{\circ}\right) *\left(4{~m}+5{~m}\left(\cos \left(50^{\circ}\right)\right)+4,000{~ N}*2{~m}=0\right. \\
& \sum F_x=F_A \cos \left(50^{\circ}\right)+C_x=0 \\
& \sum F_y=F_A \sin \left(50^{\circ}\right)-4,000{~N}+C_y=0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving the first equation for F~A~ yields F~A~ = 2,611 N.

Substituting F~A~ into the force equations yields C~x~ = -1,678 N and C~y~ = 2,000 N.

Knowing C~x~ and C~y~, we can find the overall pin force F~C~.

$$
F_C=\sqrt{C_x^2+C_y^2}=2,611{~N}
$$

Since C~x~ is negative, it points in the opposite direction from the assumed FBD. To avoid modifying the FBD, continue assuming the original direction but substitute in the negative value in further calculations.

As an alternative solution (explored in @sec-1.3), bar AB and bar BC can be separated by removing the pin at point B and drawing the FBD of bar BC as shown. (Recall that because bar AB is a two-force member, F~B~ goes through points A and B and that F~A~ = F~B~. We can solve for F~A~ by solving for F~B~.)

![](images/example%201.2%20part%203%20may%2030.png){fig-alt="Free body diagram of bar BC. A downward arrow labeled 4000 N is applied at the midpoint of the horizontal bar. Reaction forces C sub x and C sub y are shown at point C. A single force labeled F sub B is applied at point B, directed southwest along the axis of the inclined bar." fig-align="center" width="445"}

$$
\sum M_C= F_B \sin(50^\circ)*4{~m}+F_B \cos(50^\circ)*0+4,000{~N}*2{~m}=0
$$

This yields the same result: F~A~ = 2,611 N, and the rest of the problem proceeds the same way.

**Answer:**

F~A~ = 2,611 N

F~C~ = 2,611 N
:::
::::
:::::

</details>

## Internal Reactions {#sec-1.3}

<details>

<summary>Click to expand</summary>

Internal reactions can be forces and moments at connection points between members (e.g., pins connecting multiple members of a frame, machine, or truss), as well as reactions at any point in a continuous body (e.g., a point in the middle of a beam). These reactions are the forces and/or moments necessary to hold a structure or a body together and are ultimately the loading aspect needed to determine if and how a body will deform or even break.

### Internal Reactions at Pinned Connections

An FBD of an entire structure consisting of connected members omits the forces at the connecting pins because the connected members exert equal and opposite forces on each other, and these forces cancel each other in equilibrium equations. The pins that connect the members nonetheless experience reaction forces you must determine to ensure the integrity of the structure. Do the following to find the reaction forces in these internal pins:

-   Prepare the individual members' FBDs (imagine the pins pulled out and consider each member separately). With the members disconnected, the reaction forces at the connecting pins no longer cancel each other out in equilibrium equations applied to the individual members. The reaction forces are drawn on the FBDs in the same way as for support pins, described in @sec-1.1.

    -   However, since the connected members do ultimately exert equal and opposite forces on each other, the reaction forces of pins common to two members are drawn in opposite directions on these members' FBDs.

-   Given the FBDs, write and solve equilibrium equations for each body. Because three equilibrium equations can be determined for each FBD drawn, one could theoretically solve for three times the number of unknowns.

    -   Note that the FBD of the whole structure may be useful to help solve for some unknowns.

    -   In addition, as discussed, when one of the connected members is a two-force member, the reaction at the pin runs through the points where the forces act on the two-force member.

@exm-1.3 and the alternative method used for @exm-1.2 demonstrate these concepts.

::::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}
## Example 1.3

:::: {#exm-1.3}
<p>

</p>

A plant hanger is secured to a wall with a pin and is supported by a pin brace connected to the hanger at B and to the wall at C.

Determine the external reactions at A and C as well as the reaction in the internal pin B.

![](images/chapter%201%20figure%20updates/example%201.3.png){fig-alt="Plant hanger structure. A horizontal bar extends 7 feet from a vertical wall and is connected to the wall at point A with a pin support. Point B, located 3 feet to the right of A along the bar, contains an internal pin that connects to a diagonal brace. A potted plant hangs from the far right end of the horizontal bar, 7 feet from point A, with a downward arrow labeled 50 pounds representing the load. A third pin support is located at point C, 4 feet directly below point A. A diagonal brace extends upward and to the right (northeast) from point C to internal pin B on the horizontal bar." fig-align="center" width="319"}

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}
## Solution

If BC were not a two-force member, we would approach the problem by separating the brace from the hanger and drawing an FBD of each part separately. Notice that B~x~ and B~y~ are drawn in opposite directions in the two different diagrams because the pin exerts an equal and opposite force on each bar.

![](images/CH1%20PNGs/example%201.3%20part%202.png){fig-alt="Two free body diagrams of the plant hanger structure. The horizontal bar is shown on the left, and the diagonal brace on the right. On the left, reaction forces A sub x and A sub y are applied at point A, pointing left and upward. At internal pin B, reaction forces B sub x and B sub y are shown, pointing left and downward. A downward force labeled 50 pounds is applied at the far right end of the bar. On the right, the diagonal brace is shown separately, oriented northeast. Reaction forces C sub x and C sub y are applied at the lower end, pointing right and upward." fig-align="center" width="563"}

Six unknowns and six equilibrium equations (three equations per body) are now available to use per bar, so the problem is technically solvable. However, since BC is a two-force member—there is a pin force at B and a pin force at C but no other forces at any other point on the bar—the reaction force at B follows a line of action that goes through B and C. We can also conclude that the force in pin C is equal to the force in pin B. The FBD of bar AB can be redrawn.

![](images/CH1%20PNGs/example%201.3%20part%203.png){fig-alt="Free body diagram of the horizontal bar from the plant hanger structure. Forces A sub x and A sub y are applied at the left end, pointing left and upward, respectively. A downward force labeled 50 pounds is applied at the far right end. At point B, reaction forces B sub x and B sub y are replaced with a single diagonal force labeled F sub B, pointing downward and to the left (southwest). A right triangle is drawn alongside F sub B, with horizontal and vertical legs labeled 3 and 4, indicating the slope of the diagonal brace." fig-align="center" width="306"}

With the components B~x~ and B~y~ replaced with the resultant force F~B~ with known direction, the number of unknowns on bar AB is reduced to three. These unknowns can be solved for using the equilibrium equations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \sum M_A=F_B\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)*3{~ft}-50{~lb}*7{~ft}=0 \\
& \sum F_x=-A_x+F_B\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)=0 \\
& \sum F_y=A_y+F_B\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)-50{~lb}=0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving the equations yields F~B~ = 145.8 lb, A~x~ = 87.5 lb, and A~y~ = -66.7 lb. Therefore, the pin force in pin B is 145.8 lb and the pin force in A is $\mathrm{A}=\sqrt{A_x^2+A_y^2}=110{~lb}$. Since BC is a two-force member, F~C~ = F~B~.

**Answer:**

F~A~ = 110 lb

F~B~ = F~C~ = 146.8 lb
:::
::::
:::::

### Internal Reactions in Truss Structures

Truss structures are made up of only two-force members. The two main methods of determining internal reactions in planar (2D) truss structures are method of joints and method of sections. In using method of joints, we draw an FBD of the connecting pins (joints) within the truss. Since all the members connected at any given pin will be two-force members, the reactions can be drawn in known directions. However, because the forces all pass through the same point on the body, the moment equilibrium equation is not useful, so only the forces equilibrium equations can be used for each joint. This means that only two unknowns can be solved for at each joint. The method of joints is most useful when the forces of all the truss members are sought or when the only forces sought are attached to a joint with only two members.

To use method of sections, we cut through the truss structure and with the FBD analyze the intact part of the structure either to the left or to the right of the cut. The FBD of either side will show the applied forces and the reaction forces from the cut-through members. These reactions will be equal in magnitude but opposite in direction between the two sides of the cut. The side to examine is usually that which will not require finding external reactions (if there is a free end to the truss) and/or is least complicated to deal with in terms of geometry or applied loads. All three equilibrium equations can generally be effectively applied with method of sections, so three unknowns can be solved for with any given cut. Keep in mind that moments can be taken about points that lie outside the isolated section, which can lead to more efficient solutions.

@exm-1.4 demonstrates the use of method of joints and method of sections to solve for forces in truss members.

:::::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}
## Example 1.4

::::: {#exm-1.4}
<p>

</p>

Determine the forces in members DE and BE. Let F~1~ = 8 kN and F~2~ = 12 kN.

![](images/Updated%20CH1%20examples/example%201.4%20part%201.png){fig-alt="A pin-connected truss attached vertically to a wall on the left. The truss includes joints labeled A, B, C, D, E, and F, listed in counterclockwise order starting from the top left. Joint F, at the top left, is a pin connection. Joint A is 5 meters directly below F and also has a pin connection. Joint B is 2 meters to the right of A, with a downward force labeled F sub 1 Member AB connects A and B horizontally. Joint C is 5 meters to the right of B, with a downward force labeled F sub 2. Member BC connects B and C horizontally. At the top, joint E is 2 meters to the right of F, and joint D is 4 meters to the right of E. Member FE connects F and E horizontally. Vertical member AF connects A and F. Diagonal member AE connects A and E upward and to the right. Vertical member BE connects B and E. Diagonal member BD connects B and D upward and to the right. Member DC connects D and C downward and to the right. The horizontal distance between D and C is labeled as 1 meter." fig-align="center" width="546"}

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}
## Solution: Method of Joints

With method of joints, always work with a joint that has no more than two unknown forces acting on it. These unknown forces come either from members attached to the joint or from external support reactions. Joint C is a good place to start here, as there are only two unknown forces: BC and CD.

**FBD Joint C**

![](images/Updated%20CH1%20examples/example%201.4%20part%202.png){fig-alt="Free body diagram of joint C. Two force members, BC and CD, are connected at point C. Member BC exerts a force horizontally to the left. Member CD exerts a force directed upward and to the northwest. A downward external force of 12 kilonewtons (F sub 2) is applied at point C. An angle theta is marked between members BC and CD." fig-align="center" width="158"}

Angle 𝛉 can be found by setting up a right triangle at C with a base of 1 m and a height of 5 m. Once this angle is known, we can use equilibrium equations to find the forces in members BC and CD.

$$
\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{1}\right)=78.7^{\circ} \\
\\
\begin{aligned}
&\sum F_y=C D \sin \theta-12=0 \quad\rightarrow\quad C D=12.2{~kN} \\
&\sum F_x=-B C-C D \cos \theta=0 \quad\rightarrow\quad B C=-2.4{~kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the forces in the FBD are shown to be tensile—the forces are pointed away from the joint—the negative answer indicates that force BC is actually compressive.

Now that force CD is known to be 12.2 kN in tension, two unknowns remain at joint D: BD and DE.

**FBD Joint D**

![](images/chapter%201%20figure%20updates/example%201.4%20part%203.png){fig-alt="Free-body diagram of joint D where three members meet. Member DE extends horizontally to the left, member BD extends downward to the left, and member CD extends downward to the right. Arrows along each member show the forces acting at joint D. The angle between DE and BD is labeled alpha, and the angle between CD and the horizontal is labeled theta." fig-align="center" width="348"}

We know the 12.2 kN force acts at an angle of θ = 78.7° from the horizontal. Angle ⍺ can be found by considering right-angle triangle BDE, which has a base of 4 m and a height of 5 m. Once this angle is known, we can use equilibrium equations to find the forces in members BD and DE.

$$
\alpha=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{4}\right)=51.3^{\circ} \\
\\
\begin{aligned}
\sum F_y&=-B C \sin \theta-B D \sin \alpha=0 \quad \\
&=-12.2 \sin \left(78.7^{\circ}\right)-B D \sin \left(51.3^{\circ}\right)=0 \quad\rightarrow\quad B D=-15.4{~kN} \\
\sum F_x&=-D E-B D \cos \left(\alpha\right)+12.2 \cos (\theta)=0 \\
&=-D E-(-15.4\ kN) \cos \left(51.3^{\circ}\right)+12.2\ kN\cos \left(78.7^{\circ}\right)=0 \quad\rightarrow\quad D E=12{~kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

Force BD is 15.4 kN in compression and force DE is 12 kN in tension. Now we need to find force BE. Two unknowns remain at joint B: AB and BE.

**FBD Joint B**

![](images/chapter%201%20figure%20updates/example%201.4%20part%204.png){fig-alt="Free-body diagram of joint B showing four members meeting at the joint. Member AB extends horizontally to the left, BE extends vertically upward, BD extends diagonally upward to the right, and BC extends horizontally to the right. The force in AB acts to the left, the force in BE acts upward, and an external load of 8 kN acts vertically downward at B. The angle between members BD and BC is labeled alpha." fig-align="center" width="434"}

$$
\begin{aligned}\sum F_y&=B E-8+B D \sin \alpha=0 \quad \\&=B E-8+(-15.4) \sin \left(51.3^{\circ}\right)=0 \quad\rightarrow\quad B E=20{~kN} \\\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:**

DE = 12 kN (Tensile)

BE = 20 kN (Tensile)
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}
## Solution: Method of Sections

Although method of joints can be used here, it is an inefficient approach requiring us to draw and analyze FBDs of joints C, D, and B as well as to keep track of the forces at these joints and whether each force is in tension or compression.

As an alternative, we may use method of sections to find forces BE and DE. To apply this method, cut through no more than three unknown members and draw an FBD with no more than three total unknowns. For this problem, make a cut through the truss that passes through members AB, BE, and DE.

![](images/Updated%20CH1%20examples/example%201.4%20part%205.png){fig-alt="The truss with a cut section indicated by a dashed line running diagonally in the southwest direction, passing through members DE, BE, and AB. A scissors symbol is placed at the top end of the dashed line, marking the use of the method of sections to analyze internal forces." fig-align="center" width="437"}

Once that cut is made, draw an FBD for the intact part of the truss either to the left or to the right of the cut. The external forces on the chosen side and the force of each cut-through member need to be shown on the FBD.

If we chose the left side, there would be unknowns AB, BE, and DE as well as unknown reactions A~x~, F~x~, and F~y~. However, the right side would have no unknown support reactions, so the only unknowns are forces AB, BE, and DE.

![](images/Updated%20CH1%20examples/example%201.4%20part%206.png){fig-alt="The right-side section of the truss after a diagonal cut through members AB, BE, and DE. Internal forces are indicated on each member. At joint B, force AB points horizontally to the left, and force BE points vertically upward. An external force of 8 kilonewtons acts downward at joint B. At joint C, another external force of 12 kilonewtons acts vertically downward. Force DE acts horizontally to the left from joint D. The horizontal distance from B to D is labeled 4 meters, from D to C is 1 meter, and the vertical distance from D up to C is 5 meters." fig-align="center" width="450"}

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \sum M_B=-(12*5)+(DE*5)=0 \quad\rightarrow\quad D E=12{~kN} \\
& \sum F_y=BE-8-12=0 \quad\rightarrow\quad B E=20{~kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

Once again the forces are drawn on the FBD in the tensile direction. The positive answers confirm that the forces are indeed tensile.

**Answer:**

DE = 12 kN (Tensile)

BE = 20 kN (Tensile)
:::
:::::
::::::

### Internal Reactions in Continuous Bodies

Internal reactions also exist within a body or structure. These reactions are necessary to hold the body together and vary from point to point in a body depending on the distribution of external loading. As shown in @fig-1.6, the reactions at any given point can be examined by making a cut at the point of interest in the body. Think of any point within a body as acting as a fixed support for the rest of the body. That is, every point must potentially exert a force parallel to the cross section where the cut is made, which is the shear force V; a force perpendicular to the cross section where the cut is made, which is the normal force N; and a reaction moment where the cut is made, which is the bending moment M. Moreover, as discussed for internal pin reactions and the cut made for method of sections for trusses, the reactions at a cut will be equal and opposite on the two sides of the cut.

![Cross-sections showing the internal loads on a continuous body](images/CH1%20PNGs/figure%201.2.png){#fig-1.6 fig-alt="Three diagrams are presented. An uncut beam is at the top, and the left and right portions after a vertical cut are shown below. The beam is supported by a pin at point A on the left and a roller at point B on the right. Two external forces, F sub 1 and F sub 2, act diagonally F sub 1 in the southwest direction and F sub 2 in the southeast direction positioned on either side of a vertical dashed line in the middle of the beam, where the cut occurs. A scissors symbol appears at the top of the cut to indicate the cut location. The bottom-left diagram shows the left side of the beam: force F sub 1, in red, points southwest. The pin support at point A is replaced with reaction forces A sub x and A sub y, in red, pointing left and downward. At the cut on the right end, internal forces are shown in blue: shear force V acting downward, normal force N acting right, and a counterclockwise moment M. The bottom-right diagram shows the right side of the beam: force F sub 2, in red, points southeast. The roller at point B is replaced with an upward reaction force, in red. At the cut on the left end, internal forces in blue are: shear force V acting upward, normal force N acting left, and a counterclockwise moment M." fig-align="center" width="483"}

To determine the reactions, draw and use the FBD of the part of the beam either to the left or to the right of the cut. As with method of sections for trusses, choose which side of the cut to examine primarily by determining which side appears easiest and most efficient to analyze. Once the FBD of the cut section is drawn, the three equilibrium equations can be applied to determine the internal reactions. The determination of shear and bending moments in beams is reviewed in more detail in @sec-beams. The focus of @exm-1.5 is on the determination of the normal force, as this is important in @sec-stress and @sec-strain.

::::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}
## Example 1.5

:::: {#exm-1.5}
<p>

</p>

Two solid bars make up the axial assembly loaded as shown.

Determine the normal force in each bar. State whether the force is tensile or compressive.

![](images/Updated%20CH1%20examples/example%201.5%20part%201.png){fig-alt="A horizontal assembly consisting of two connected solid bars, labeled A to B and B to C, with a fixed support at point C. A leftward force of 50 kilonewtons is applied at the left end of bar A, and a rightward force of 180 kilonewtons is applied at point B." fig-align="center" width="429"}

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}
## Solution

Though the assembly is not a beam, determining the internal reactions works in the same way. In this particular case, all the forces are in the normal direction (no shear force). Furthermore, because of the central placement of the 50 kN and 180 kN forces, there is no bending moment. Consequently, only the normal reaction force is drawn on the FBDs.

To find the internal load in a segment, cut a cross-section within that segment. Since the external loading (and therefore the internal loading) changes at point B, make one cut in segment AB and another in segment BC.

Making the cut in section AB and drawing the FBD allow us to determine the normal force in section AB. Drawing the FBD of the left section of the cut allows us to avoid needing to know the external reactions at wall C.

![](images/Updated%20CH1%20examples/example%201.5%20part%202.png){fig-alt="Free body diagram of the left segment of cylindrical bar AB. A leftward force of 50 kilonewtons is applied at the left end. A vertical dashed line indicates a cut made to the right of point A, anywhere along AB but before point B. An internal normal force labeled N sub AB acts rightward at the cut." fig-align="center" width="199"}

$$
\sum F_x=-50{~kN}+N_{AB}=0 \quad\rightarrow\quad N_{AB}=50{~kN}
$$

Making the cut in section BC and drawing the FBD allows us to determine the normal force in section BC. Once again draw the side to the left of the cut to avoid needing the reaction at the wall.

![](images/Updated%20CH1%20examples/example%201.5%20part%203.png){fig-alt="Free body diagram of the left segment of two connected cylindrical bars, including member AB and part of member BC. A leftward force of 50 kilonewtons is applied at point A, and a rightward force of 180 kilonewtons acts at point B. A vertical dashed line marks a cut made to the right of point B, anywhere along BC but before point C. An internal normal force labeled N sub BC acts rightward at the cut." fig-align="center" width="303"}

$$
\sum F_x=-50{~kN}+180{~kN}+N_{BC}=0 \quad\rightarrow\quad N_{BC}=-130{~kN}
$$

For both AB and BC, the internal normal force was assumed tensile in the FBD and equilibrium equation. In the case of N~AB~, the positive answer confirms that it is tensile. In the case of N~BC~, the negative answer reveals that it is compressive.

**Answer:**

N~AB~ = 50 kN (Tensile)

N~BC~ = 130 kN (Compressive)
:::
::::
:::::

</details>

## Equilibrium and Reactions in Three Dimensions {#sec-1.4}

<details>

<summary>Click to expand</summary>

While we can often model structures as two dimensional, as in the previous examples, many real-life structures are subjected to forces and moments in all directions, necessitating the consideration of three-dimensional forces and three-dimensional moments. The process of finding external and internal reactions is the same in 3D as in 2D, but there are three additional equilibrium equations and three additional reactions.

For 3D systems there are six total scalar equilibrium equations as shown.

$$
\boxed{\begin{array}{ll}
\sum F_x=0 &\;\;\; \sum M_x=0 \\
\sum F_y=0 &\;\;\; \sum M_y=0 \\
\sum F_z=0 &\;\;\; \sum M_z=0
\end{array}}
$$ {#eq-1.2}

Recall that the moment vector describes the axis around which the body tends to rotate. Each individual component represents the tendency of the body to rotate around the specified axis.

In 3D there are three internal forces and three internal moments (@fig-1.7). One normal force (N~x~) is perpendicular to the cross-section, and two shear forces (V~y~ and V~z~) are parallel to the cross-section. Two bending moments (M~y~ and M~z~) act around the axes parallel to the cross-section, and one torsional moment (T~x~) acts around the axis perpendicular to the cross-section. This textbook examines each of these loads.

![In 3D there are three internal forces (normal force N~x~ and two shear forces V~y~ and V~z~) and three internal moments (torsional moment T~x~ and two bending moments M~y~ and M~z~).](images/CH1%20PNGs/figure%201.3.png){#fig-1.7 fig-alt="A horizontal rectangular solid block oriented along the x-, y-, and z-axes. A 3D coordinate system is shown separately on the left. At the right end of the block, a cross-sectional cut reveals internal forces and moments: a normal force N sub x acts rightward along the positive x-axis; a shear force V sub y acts upward along the positive y-axis; a shear force V sub z acts outward along the positive z-axis; a torsional moment T sub x acts counterclockwise about the x-axis; a bending moment M sub y acts counterclockwise about the y-axis; and a bending moment M sub z acts counterclockwise about the z-axis." fig-align="center" width="510"}

Note that here the choice of coordinate system is arbitrary (as long as it is a right handed coordinate system). Loads are defined as normal, shear, bending, or torsional according to the effect they have on the body. For example, the normal force always acts in the direction perpendicular to the cross-section of the body and has the effect of extending or compressing the body. In the case of @fig-1.7, the x-axis is the axis perpendicular to the cross-section, so the force in the x direction is the normal force. If the axes or the body were oriented differently, the x component of the force could be a shear force instead of a normal force.

While summing the reaction forces in 3D is a straightforward process of adding forces in each direction, summing moments in 3D can prove to be more complicated. To sum moments, there are generally two options. One option is to use the cross product to calculate moments: $\vec M=\vec r \times \vec F$.

### Calculating Moments

Any position vector that goes from the point the moment is about to any point on the line of action of the force (F) will work for $\vec{r}$ in the cross product. Considering @fig-1.8, we could say

$$
\begin{aligned}\vec{M_O}&=\vec{r_1}\times\vec{F}\\&=\vec{r_2}\times\vec{F}\\&=\vec{r_3}\times\vec{F}\end{aligned}\\
$$

Using the cross product to calculate moment will result in a vector expression for the moment equation that gives all three components at one time with the correct signs to indicate clockwise (negative) or counterclockwise (positive) rotation. The direction of rotation depends on the perspective, here taken from the positive end of the axis looking toward the negative.

The second option to calculate moments is to perform scalar calculations in which the sum of the moments about the x-, y-, and z-axis is calculated individually. To use this option, it might be helpful to recall several concepts:

1.  The general scalar equation for moment is M = F⨉d, where d is the perpendicular distance between the point the moment is being taken about and the line of action of the force (see @fig-1.8). You can also use

    $$M=Fr\sin (\alpha),$$

    where

    *r = any straight-line distance from the point the moment is being taken about to any point on the force vector* $\vec F$

    *α = angle between* $\vec F$ *and the position vector* $\vec{r}$ *that corresponds to the distance r.*

    @fig-1.8 shows various α and r pairs. Note that either α or its complement angle can be used because the sine of the angle is the same either way.

2.  Forces do not cause moments about points they traverse or axes they act through.

3.  Forces do not cause moments about axes they are parallel to—for example, F~x~ wouldn't cause a moment around an x-axis no matter where the point is.

4.  When taking the moment about a point, move the origin of the coordinate axes to that point to determine the distance between the axis and the force.

![The moment around O due to the force (F) can be calculated using M = r sin(⍺), M = Fd, or $\vec M = \vec r\times\vec F$.](images/figure%201.4.png){#fig-1.8 fig-alt="Point O is located above a blue force vector pointing northeast. Three blue position vectors: r sub 1, r sub 2, and r sub 3 , extend outward from point O to different points along the line of action of the force vector. r sub 1 and r sub 2 point southwest, and r sub 3 points southeast. A red perpendicular line labeled d extends from point O to the force vector, with a right angle marked. Angles between each position vector and the force vector are labeled: alpha sub 1 (between F and r sub 1), alpha sub 2 (between F and r sub 2), and alpha sub 3 (between F and r sub 3)." fig-align="center" width="493"}

Given all the foregoing reminders, you can apply the following equations:

$\sum M_x= \pm F_y * z \pm F_z * y$, where z and y are the distances from the x-axis at the point in question to F~y~ and F~z~ respectively.

$\sum M_y= \pm F_x * z \pm F_z * x$, where z and x are the distances from the y-axis at the point in question to F~x~ and F~z~ respectively.

$\sum M_z= \pm F_x * y \pm F_y * x$, where y and x are the distances from the z-axis at the point in question to the F~x~ and F~y~ respectively.

The ± is decided by the right-hand rule or visual inspection. As mentioned, when using visual inspection, judge the direction of rotation by looking from the positive end of the axis toward the negative end. A counterclockwise rotation is considered positive, and a clockwise rotation is considered negative.

Take these steps to apply the right-hand rule:

1.  Orient your right hand so that the palm is at the moment point and the fingers are aligned with the moment arm, extended to the force. The moment arm is the axis along which the perpendicular distance to the axis would be determined. For example, if finding M~x~ due to F~y~, the moment arm is in the z direction.

2.  The thumb is aligned with the axis of rotation—that is, with the axis around which the moment is being calculated.

3.  Curl your fingers in the direction of the force. If to perform this action your thumb is pointed in the positive rotation axis direction, the moment is counterclockwise. If your thumb is pointed in the negative rotation axis direction, the moment is clockwise. Typical convention designates counterclockwise rotation to be positive and clockwise to be negative.

![Illustration of applying right hand rule](images/figure%201.5.png){#fig-1.9 fig-alt="Diagram illustrating the right-hand rule for moments. On the left, a 3D coordinate system with dashed x, y, and z axes is shown. A label reads “thumb pointed outward = positive.” On the right, two side-view illustrations of a right hand demonstrate how to determine the direction of a moment. The hand on the far right is oriented diagonally upward to the right, with point O located at the base of the thumb. A diagonal line labeled “moment arm” extends from point O along the hand, and a force vector labeled “Force” points to the left, perpendicular to the moment arm. Between the two hands, a curved arrow labeled “curl in direction of force” shows the fingers curling counterclockwise. The thumb points outward, indicating the positive moment direction according to the right-hand rule." fig-align="center" width="381"}

These concepts are further reviewed in @exm-1.6.

::::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}
## Example 1.6

:::: {#exm-1.6}
<p>

</p>

Determine the internal reactions at point P, located at the center of the cross-section of the rectangular bar and 1.75 ft from the fixed support at the origin. Let **F** = -150**i** - 225**j** + 300**k** lb.

![](images/chapter%201%20figure%20updates/example%201.6%20part%201.png){fig-alt="A 3D gray rectangular bar oriented along the green positive x-axis, with all coordinate axes (x, y, z) shown in dashed lines. The bar is 3 feet long along the x-axis. A yellow rectangular cross-section lies in the yz-plane, located 1.75 feet to the right of the origin, with point P marked at its center. A force vector F (with a bar over the letter) is applied at the top-back-right corner of the bar, 3 feet to the right of the origin, pointing southwest. On the right, a 2D view of the yellow cross-section is shown with the z-axis pointing left and the y-axis pointing upward. The cross-section is 6 inches wide along the z-axis and 4 inches tall along the y-axis, with point P centered." fig-align="center" width="481"}

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" icon="false"}
## Solution

To determine the internal reactions at P, a cut is made at P that is parallel to the cross section. The section to the left of the cut includes the wall but the section to the right of the cut has no support. We will draw the FBD of the section to the right of the cut to avoid needing to know the reaction at the wall.

![](images/example%201.6%20part%202.png){fig-alt="A 3D rectangular bar cut at point P, showing the free body diagram of the right segment. The global coordinate system is shown with green dashed lines: the x-axis extends to the right, the y-axis points upward, and the z-axis projects outward toward the viewer. Internal forces and moments are illustrated on the cut face. At point P, blue arrows represent internal forces: P sub x pointing left, P sub y pointing upward, and P sub z pointing inward (negative z-direction). Internal moments are also shown in blue: M sub Px acting clockwise about the x-axis, M sub Py counterclockwise about the y-axis, and M sub Pz counterclockwise about the z-axis. A red external force vector, labeled with a bar over F, is applied in the northwest direction." fig-align="center" width="386"}

The x direction is normal to the cut section, so P~x~ is the internal normal force reaction. P~y~ and P~z~ are the internal shear force reactions.

The force reactions P~x~, P~y~, and P~z~ at P can be found by applying the force equilibrium equations.

$$
\begin{aligned}\sum F_x &= -P_x + F_x = 0\\&=-P_x-150\ lb=0\quad\rightarrow\quad P_x = -150 lb\end{aligned}
$$

P~x~ was assumed tensile (pointed away from the cut section), so the negative answer here means the force is compressive.

$$\begin{aligned}\sum F_y &= P_y + F_y = 0\\&=P_y-225\ lb=0\quad\rightarrow\quad P_y = 225 ~lb\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}\sum F_z &= -P_z + F_z = 0\\&=-P_z+300\ lb=0\quad\rightarrow\quad P_z = 300 ~lb\end{aligned}$$

Note that shear direction forces are not designated as "tensile" or "compressive" since shear forces do not extend or compress a body. Signs on shear forces are discussed in @sec-14.1.

The internal moment reactions M~Px~, M~Py~, and M~Pz~ can be found by taking the moments about point P. The sign on the individual multiplicative terms in each equation are determined by the right-hand rule or visualization.

The moment about the x-axis at point P is

$$
\sum M_{Px} = \pm ~F_yz \pm F_zy = 0 = -[(225{~lb})(3{~in.})(\frac{1{~ft}}{12{~in.}})]+[(300{~lb})(2{~in.})(\frac{1{~ft}}{12{~in.}})]-M_{Px}=0\\[20pt]
M_{Px}= -6.25{~lb·ft}
$$

The reaction M~Px~ is subtracted because it is drawn on the FBD as counterclockwise around the negative x-axis, which means it is assumed clockwise around the positive x-axis. The negative answer means the reaction moment is opposite of the assumed direction, so it is actually counterclockwise around the positive x-axis.

The moment about the y-axis at point P is

$$
\sum M_{Py} = \pm ~F_xz \pm F_zx = 0 = (150{~lb})(3{~in.})(\frac{1{~ft}}{12{~in.}})-[(300{~lb})(1.25{~ft})]+M_{Py}=0\\[20pt]
M_{Py}= 337.5{~lb·ft}
$$

The reaction moment M~Py~ is added because it is drawn counterclockwise around the positive y-axis. The positive answer means it is actually counterclockwise.

The moment about the z-axis at point P is

$$
\sum M_{Pz} = \pm F_xy \pm F_yx = 0 = [(150{~lb})(2{~in})(\frac{1{~ft}}{12{~in}})]-[(225{~lb})(1.25{~ft})]-M_{Pz}=0\\[20pt]
M_{Pz}= -256.25{~lb·ft}
$$

Like M~Px~, the reaction moment M~Pz~ is subtracted because it is drawn on the FBD as counterclockwise around the negative z-axis. The negative answer means the reaction is actually counterclockwise around the positive z-axis.

**Answer:**

P~x~ = 150 lb (compressive)

P~y~ = 225 lb

P~z~ = 300 lb

M~Px~ = 6.25 lb·ft (counterclockwise)

M~Py~ = 337.5 lb·ft (counterclockwise)

M~Pz~ = 256.25 lb·ft (counterclockwise)
:::
::::
:::::

</details>

## Summary {.unnumbered}

<details>

<summary>Click to expand</summary>

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Key Takeaways

Bodies in this text are in static equilibrium and subjected to external forces and moments which can cause deformations. In order to determine the type and extent of deformation, we will need to understand how to calculate external and internal reactions to loading.

External reactions can be found by drawing FBDs of the whole structure and applying equilibrium equations.

Internal reactions can be found by separating parts (for structures made of connected members) or making cuts (for solid bodies or truss structures when method of sections is used) and applying equilibrium to the separated parts or sections.

In the case of connected members, it can be helpful to the process to recognize two-force members and understand how they simplify analysis.

The effects of these internal loads on deformable bodies is the focus of this text.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Key Equations

Static equilibrium:

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\sum F_x=0 & \sum M_x=0 \\
\sum F_y=0 & \sum M_y=0 \\
\sum F_z=0 & \sum M_z=0
\end{array}
$$

2D scalar moment equations:

$$M=(F)(d)\\M=Fr\sin \alpha\\$$

3D scalar moment equations:

$$\sum M_x = \pm F_z*y \pm F_y*z\\\sum M_y = \pm F_z*x \pm F_x*z\\\sum M_z = \pm F_x*y \pm F_y*x\\$$

Vector moment equation:

$$\vec{M}=\vec{r}\times\vec{F}\\$$

Force magnitude:

$$F = \sqrt{F_x^2+F_y^2}$$
:::

</details>

## References {.unnumbered}

<details>

<summary>Click to expand</summary>

**Figures**

All figures in this chapter were created by Kindred Grey in 2025 and released under a CC BY license.

</details>
